network.overall_strategy: This network is an explicitly literature-constrained hypothesis graph for prebiotic tryptophan centered on the experimentally supported bottleneck that indole formation is unresolved on Earth. The network therefore uses only routes documented in the supplied literature: (i) hydrothermal serpentinization and iron-sulfur carbon fixation to produce simple carbon and amino-acid precursors, (ii) surface Strecker and formose chemistry to produce serine and related intermediates, (iii) hydrothermal pyrrole formation from acetylene and ammonia, (iv) interstellar/exogenous delivery of indole to the early Earth, and (v) two conditional tryptophan-forming branches recognized in the literature: the Ménez Fe-rich saponite Friedel-Crafts hypothesis and the theoretically sound Strecker route from indole-3-acetaldehyde. The 10 listed pathways are alternative traversals through a single connected bipartite graph; several are explicitly marked speculative where the literature says no demonstrated prebiotic reaction exists.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formate on greigite/mackinawite
reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface hydrogenation of CO2 on Fe-S sites gives surface-bound reduced C1 species released as formate.
reactions.reasoning: Supported by iron-sulfur world and hydrothermal vent studies showing CO2 reduction on FeS/Fe3S4-type minerals and favorable vent thermodynamics; used here as the network's conservative C1 entry step under alkaline hydrothermal conditions (Russell & Hall, 1997; Martin & Russell, 2007; White et al., 2015; Santos-Carballal et al., 2018; Amend & Shock, 1998).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal C1 reduction from formate to formaldehyde

conditions.other: subseafloor reducing conditions; treated as a connecting reduction step

reactions.mechanism: Further reduction of a C1 carboxylate equivalent on iron-bearing mineral surfaces affords a more reduced aldehyde-level carbon intermediate.
reactions.reasoning: This is a conservative connector motivated by hydrothermal CO2 hydrogenation/Fischer-Tropsch-type chemistry on iron minerals; the exact formate-to-formaldehyde conversion is less directly demonstrated than formate formation and is included as a necessary but partially speculative link to literature-supported sugar chemistry (Amend & Shock, 1998; White et al., 2015; Santos-Carballal et al., 2018).

reactions.name: Formose entry: formaldehyde to glycolaldehyde

conditions.other: evaporitic pool or geothermal field, wet-dry concentration, anoxic

reactions.mechanism: Base/mineral-assisted carbonyl coupling within formose-like chemistry generates the C2 sugar aldehyde; borate helps stabilize carbohydrate products.
reactions.reasoning: Grounded in classic formose chemistry from formaldehyde and the literature emphasis that glyceraldehyde/serine precursors arise from formose-type networks, with borate stabilization of sugars in surface settings (Butlerow, 1861; Breslow, 1959; Li et al., 2024).

reactions.name: Aldol extension to glyceraldehyde

conditions.other: wet-dry cycles in evaporitic pools; borate stabilizes trioses

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-assisted aldol/formose extension converts C1 plus C2 carbonyl compounds into the C3 triose glyceraldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: Literature-guided by the role of formose chemistry in generating glyceraldehyde, a documented serine precursor, with borate acting as a stabilizer in surface environments (Butlerow, 1861; Breslow, 1959; Li et al., 2024).

reactions.name: Surface Strecker synthesis and hydrolysis to serine from glycolaldehyde

conditions.other: aqueous Strecker conditions followed by hydrolysis during wet-dry cycling

reactions.mechanism: Glycolaldehyde forms an imine with ammonia, cyanide adds to give the aminonitrile, and subsequent hydrolysis yields serine.
reactions.reasoning: Serine is one of the few tryptophan-relevant precursors with multiple plausible prebiotic origins. A Strecker route from glycolaldehyde/glyceraldehyde with HCN and NH3 is specifically highlighted in the report as plausible for serine (Kirschning, 2022; Li et al., 2024).

reactions.name: Surface Strecker synthesis and hydrolysis to serine from glyceraldehyde

conditions.other: aqueous Strecker conditions with later hydrolysis during thermal cycling

reactions.mechanism: Glyceraldehyde enters Strecker chemistry via imine formation and cyanide addition, followed by nitrile hydrolysis to serine.
reactions.reasoning: The deep research explicitly notes serine can form prebiotically by Strecker synthesis from glycolaldehyde or glyceraldehyde with HCN and NH3; this is one of the best-supported surface routes in the network (Kirschning, 2022; Li et al., 2024).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal pyruvate synthesis from CO2 and H2
reactions.mechanism: Stepwise mineral-catalyzed reductive carbon fixation yields a C3 α-keto acid analog of proto-metabolic pyruvate.
reactions.reasoning: Included because hydrothermal α-keto-acid formation is central to the supplied framework and consistent with vent-based proto-metabolic chemistry, though not specific to tryptophan in the literature packet (Amend & Shock, 1998; Russell & Hall, 1997; Martin & Russell, 2007).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal reductive amination of pyruvate to alanine

conditions.other: anoxic hydrothermal setting; mineral-assisted reductive amination

reactions.mechanism: The α-keto acid is aminated by ammonia and reduced on iron-sulfur mineral surfaces to the corresponding amino acid.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrothermal reductive amination of α-keto acids is part of the required framework and consistent with the report's emphasis on pyrite/Fe-S minerals promoting amino-acid synthesis and photochemical/semiconducting amino-acid formation (Amend & Shock, 1998; Nature Communications, 2024).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal pyrrole formation from acetylene and ammonia

conditions.catalyst: sulfide mineral surfaces in volcanic hydrothermal setting
conditions.other: anaerobic aqueous conditions; literature reports NiS/CoS specifically, represented here within required hydrothermal sulfide-mineral schema

reactions.mechanism: Acetylene undergoes sulfide-mineral-assisted oligomerization/cyclization with ammonia incorporation to form a five-membered N-heteroaromatic ring.
reactions.reasoning: Directly grounded in the report's citation that pyrrole forms from acetylene and ammonia under aqueous anaerobic hydrothermal conditions at 105C over sulfide catalysts, establishing a realistic indole-precursor branch (Seel et al., 2021). Because the schema restricts hydrothermal agents to listed Fe-sulfide minerals, chemically analogous vent sulfide surfaces are specified while preserving the reported environment and temperature.

reactions.name: Surface acetylene aromatization to benzene

conditions.temperature: 400C local thermal event or UV-driven surface photochemistry
conditions.other: subaerial volcanic or high-energy surface setting; included as aromatic ring source, not as demonstrated tryptophan chemistry

reactions.mechanism: Acetylene oligomerization/trimerization under energetic conditions yields benzene as an aromatic C6 fragment.
reactions.reasoning: The report notes benzene formation from simple precursors is documented through acetylene trimerization under UV/electrical discharge or high temperatures around 400C, and Kaiser-related work supports aromatic ring formation from acetylene in astrophysical contexts. This step is included only as a contextual aromatic precursor and is not claimed to solve indole synthesis (Kleimeier et al., 2022; Kaiser et al., 2024).

reactions.name: Indole availability by exogenous delivery or unresolved terrestrial annulation

conditions.temperature: variable; exogenous delivery or unverified annulation conditions
conditions.other: This node represents literature-recognized indole availability rather than a demonstrated terrestrial mineral reaction

reactions.mechanism: Operationally, indole enters the network either via extraterrestrial delivery after interstellar ice synthesis or by a still-undemonstrated terrestrial ring-annulation process from simpler aromatic/N-heteroaromatic precursors.
reactions.reasoning: The literature is explicit that terrestrial prebiotic indole synthesis has not been demonstrated, while indole formation from acetylene and ammonia in interstellar ices is experimentally established. This reaction is therefore an intentionally marked placeholder/convergence node for indole availability, not a claim of solved Earth chemistry (Kaiser et al., 2024; Ménez et al., 2018; Kirschning, 2022).

reactions.name: Formation of indole-3-acetaldehyde from indole and C2 carbonyl feedstock

conditions.other: speculative prebiotic carbonyl elaboration; included only to connect the documented theoretical Strecker route

reactions.mechanism: Indole C3 electrophilic substitution/carbonyl elaboration yields the indole-3-ethyl aldehyde framework required for Strecker chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: The report states that Strecker synthesis from indole-3-acetaldehyde is theoretically sound but that indole-3-acetaldehyde has no known prebiotic source. This reaction is therefore explicitly speculative and included only because the literature identifies this intermediate as the key entry to the Strecker branch (Kirschning, 2022).

reactions.name: Strecker synthesis of tryptophan aminonitrile from indole-3-acetaldehyde

conditions.other: aqueous Strecker conditions in concentrated pools

reactions.mechanism: Indole-3-acetaldehyde forms an imine with ammonia; cyanide attack gives the corresponding α-aminonitrile.
reactions.reasoning: This is the literature-recognized chemically sound route toward tryptophan once indole-3-acetaldehyde is available. The report specifically identifies this transformation as theoretically sound though lacking a prebiotic source for the aldehyde precursor (Kirschning, 2022).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of tryptophan aminonitrile to tryptophan

conditions.other: aqueous hydrolysis during geothermal or wet-dry cycling

reactions.mechanism: Sequential nitrile hydration and hydrolysis convert the aminonitrile to the α-amino acid.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrolysis of aminonitriles to amino acids is standard Strecker completion chemistry and is the least problematic part of the theoretical indole-3-acetaldehyde route to tryptophan (Kirschning, 2022).

reactions.name: Fe-rich saponite nanopore Friedel-Crafts-type tryptophan formation

conditions.other: alkaline serpentinization environment, strongly reducing, H2-rich, confined clay nanoreactors

reactions.mechanism: Confinement in Fe-rich saponite nanopores promotes a Friedel-Crafts-type C3 alkylation of indole by an alanyl-equivalent side chain under reducing hydrothermal conditions.
reactions.reasoning: This reaction encodes the central Ménez hypothesis: abiotic tryptophan identified in Lost City-type serpentinites is proposed to arise by Fe-saponite-catalyzed Friedel-Crafts alkylation after indole generation in a serpentinization setting. The exact molecular sequence is unresolved and unreplicated, so this node is evidence-based but mechanistically conditional rather than laboratory-demonstrated (Ménez et al., 2018; Proskurowski et al., 2008; Russell & Hall, 1997; Martin & Russell, 2007).

reactions.name: Conditional indole-serine condensation to tryptophan in confined prebiotic microenvironments

conditions.other: requires confinement or membranes/mineral interfaces to retain hydrophobic indole; highly speculative

reactions.mechanism: A nonenzymatic analogue of the modern tryptophan synthase logic is posited in which indole reacts with a serine-derived electrophilic intermediate to furnish tryptophan.
reactions.reasoning: Included only as a speculative connector because the report explicitly states that direct prebiotic coupling of indole with serine has not been demonstrated and is kinetically problematic, especially because even biology uses substrate channeling to manage free indole. Its inclusion addresses the literature's identified experimental frontier rather than claiming support (Palazzolo & Bhatt, 2020; Kirschning, 2022; Ménez et al., 2018).

pathways.name: Ménez hydrothermal route via alanine-side-chain Friedel-Crafts capture
pathways.description: Hydrothermal carbon fixation produces pyruvate and alanine, while indole is available through the unresolved-but-supported hydrothermal/exogenous branch; Fe-rich saponite nanopores then provide the literature-based hydrothermal tryptophan hypothesis.

pathways.name: Exogenous indole plus hydrothermal alanine route
pathways.description: A simplified traversal emphasizing that indole may arrive exogenously while alanine is formed hydrothermally, converging in Fe-saponite nanopores.

pathways.name: Indole-3-acetaldehyde Strecker route from glycolaldehyde branch
pathways.description: Hydrothermal C1 chemistry feeds surface sugar chemistry; glycolaldehyde is also used as the carbonyl feedstock for the speculative formation of indole-3-acetaldehyde, then the literature-recognized Strecker route reaches tryptophan.

pathways.name: Full surface serine plus indole-3-acetaldehyde branch
pathways.description: A longer route showing hydrothermal C1 production, surface formose chemistry, serine synthesis, and the parallel indole-3-acetaldehyde Strecker branch before final hydrolysis.

pathways.name: Glycolaldehyde-derived serine with speculative direct indole condensation
pathways.description: Hydrothermal carbon fixation supports surface sugar and serine synthesis; indole availability then converges with serine in a highly speculative nonenzymatic condensation.

pathways.name: Glyceraldehyde-derived serine with speculative direct indole condensation
pathways.description: Alternative serine branch using glyceraldehyde as Strecker precursor before convergence with indole.

pathways.name: Pyrrole-led indole branch into indole-3-acetaldehyde Strecker chemistry
pathways.description: Hydrothermal pyrrole formation from acetylene and ammonia feeds the unresolved indole bottleneck, then enters the conditional aldehyde/Strecker route.

pathways.name: Shortest conditional Strecker path from supplied indole
pathways.description: A minimal route reflecting the report's statement that once indole-3-acetaldehyde exists, the Strecker path is chemically sound.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal-to-surface integrated route with both sugar and pyrrole convergence
pathways.description: Shows environmental interconnection explicitly: hydrothermal C1 chemistry feeds surface sugar chemistry while hydrothermal acetylene-ammonia chemistry feeds pyrrole; both converge at indole and then at tryptophan through the Strecker branch.

pathways.name: Dual-convergence route through serine and hydrothermal saponite nanoreactors
pathways.description: Hydrothermal pyruvate/alanine chemistry and surface serine chemistry are both represented, but the final target is assigned to the Fe-saponite hydrothermal route because that is the only direct evidence-bearing tryptophan context in the literature.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway is anchored by the Ménez hydrothermal hypothesis and reasonably uses hydrothermal carbon fixation and alanine formation. Pyrrole formation from acetylene and ammonia is literature-supported under hydrothermal-like catalytic conditions. However, the critical weakness is rxn_011: terrestrial annulation from pyrrole/benzene to indole is not demonstrated prebiotically and is explicitly identified in the literature as the main unresolved bottleneck. The final Friedel-Crafts-type tryptophan-forming step is plausible only as a hypothesis derived from the Atlantis Massif observation, not as a replicated synthesis. Thus the pathway is coherent as a literature-constrained hypothesis but not strongly validated chemically.
suggestions: Separate exogenous indole delivery from unresolved terrestrial indole annulation instead of merging them in a single reaction conceptually.
Explicitly include Fe-rich saponite as the mineral catalyst in the final Friedel-Crafts step and constrain temperature to Lost City-like moderate hydrothermal conditions.
Downgrade claims of target achievement because the key indole-forming step is currently unsupported experimentally on Earth.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is one of the cleaner pathways because it avoids requiring an Earth-based indole synthesis and instead assumes exogenous indole availability, which is consistent with the Kaiser literature. Alanine production from pyruvate under hydrothermal reducing conditions is plausible. The bottleneck remains the final Fe-saponite-mediated attachment of the amino-acid side chain to indole, which is inspired by natural occurrence evidence rather than direct laboratory demonstration. This pathway best reflects the literature's strongest supported ingredients while remaining conditional.
suggestions: Treat indole as an external input node sourced from exogenous delivery rather than as a product of rxn_011 for this pathway.
Specify the nanoporous Fe-rich saponite microenvironment and moderate temperatures below 200 C more clearly.
Acknowledge that tryptophan formation remains inferential from field detection rather than experimentally replicated.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The pathway uses credible hydrothermal-to-surface flow to reach glycolaldehyde and serine, and the Strecker/hydrolysis endgame from an aldehyde precursor is chemically sensible. The dominant issue is rxn_012: formation of indole-3-acetaldehyde from indole plus glycolaldehyde is not established in the provided literature and the literature explicitly states that indole-3-acetaldehyde lacks a known prebiotic source. Therefore the pathway is chemically coherent only after insertion of a major speculative step.
suggestions: Mark rxn_012 as highly speculative and not literature-demonstrated.
Provide a more defensible source of formaldehyde and glycolaldehyde if relying on hydrothermal C1 chemistry.
Consider representing indole-3-acetaldehyde as an externally supplied hypothetical intermediate rather than a derived product.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This longer branch has good network logic, with formaldehyde feeding formose chemistry, glyceraldehyde branching, then Strecker synthesis to serine. The end-stage Strecker conversion to tryptophan is reasonable if indole-3-acetaldehyde exists. But the literature specifically identifies that precursor as lacking a known prebiotic origin, making the crucial bridge highly speculative. The pathway is topologically strong but chemically weakened by the missing source of the aromatic aldehyde.
suggestions: Reduce confidence assigned to the indole-3-acetaldehyde branch.
Clarify whether the glyceraldehyde-derived serine branch is intended to improve yield or just network redundancy.
Avoid implying experimental support for aromatic aldehyde generation from indole plus sugar precursors.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The precursor generation to serine is reasonable, but the direct condensation of indole with serine to give tryptophan in a prebiotic setting is highly problematic. The literature highlights that even biology requires tryptophan synthase with substrate channeling to mediate this transformation, emphasizing how challenging free indole-serine coupling is in open solution. Without a compelling catalytic or confinement mechanism, this pathway is too speculative for approval.
suggestions: Do not treat direct indole-serine condensation as more than a speculative placeholder.
If retained, add a concrete confinement/catalyst rationale such as clay nanopores or vesicle partitioning and still score it as low-confidence.
Prefer the indole-3-acetaldehyde Strecker branch over this direct condensation route if a speculative branch must be included.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway is similar to P5 but uses the glyceraldehyde-serine branch. Upstream chemistry is acceptable, but the terminal step remains the core failure: there is no demonstrated prebiotic analogue of the tryptophan synthase indole + serine condensation. The literature in fact underscores why this reaction is difficult even in biology. As such, the pathway is connected but not chemically convincing.
suggestions: Retain this only as a low-confidence exploratory branch.
Explicitly note that biological substrate channeling has no clear prebiotic analogue here.
Prioritize branches tied to field evidence or exogenous indole rather than unsupported direct condensation.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway is dominated by two unresolved aromatic steps in succession: pyrrole/benzene to indole and indole to indole-3-acetaldehyde. While pyrrole formation from acetylene and ammonia has support, and benzene formation from acetylene is plausible, the fusion to indole is precisely the missing prebiotic transformation highlighted by the literature. Combining two speculative aromatic steps makes this branch especially weak.
suggestions: Do not present this as a competitive route to target under current literature constraints.
Split astrophysical indole formation from terrestrial aromatic chemistry to better reflect evidence.
Flag the annulation step as the principal unresolved bottleneck.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: As a shortest conditional route from supplied indole, this pathway is internally simple and the Strecker/hydrolysis logic is solid once indole-3-acetaldehyde is granted. But that grant is the problem: the literature specifically singles out indole-3-acetaldehyde as lacking a known prebiotic source and being unstable. This branch is therefore a useful theoretical minimal path, but not a well-supported prebiotic pathway.
suggestions: Represent this as a hypothetical testable route rather than a plausible established pathway.
State clearly that the bottleneck is generation and persistence of indole-3-acetaldehyde.
Consider adding a mineral-assisted oxidation/rearrangement hypothesis only if explicitly labeled speculative.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is one of the best topological integrations in the network, combining hydrothermal carbon fixation, surface sugar chemistry, and hydrothermal pyrrole formation before converging on aromatic and amino-acid branches. It demonstrates strong environmental interconnection. However, the same literature bottlenecks remain: unresolved indole annulation and unsupported indole-3-acetaldehyde formation. Excellent network design does not overcome the weak chemical support at these convergence points.
suggestions: Keep this as a network-integration showcase, but lower confidence for actual prebiotic realization.
Differentiate firmly between plausible precursor generation and unresolved aromatic elaboration.
Use exogenous indole input if seeking a more literature-aligned integrated pathway.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This dual-convergence design is network-rich, merging serine and alanine-related branches with indole-centered chemistry in a hydrothermal confinement scenario. The issue is that it combines one conditional literature-based target step (Fe-saponite Friedel-Crafts hypothesis) with another highly speculative one (direct indole-serine condensation). That weakens the path despite good connectivity and environmental logic.
suggestions: Prefer one target-forming hypothesis per pathway rather than stacking multiple speculative final steps.
If using the Ménez interpretation, omit the direct indole-serine condensation branch from the same path.
Improve literature alignment by framing this pathway as an exploratory convergence scenario rather than a likely route.

evaluation.network_feedback: The network is strong topologically and unusually honest about the literature constraints surrounding prebiotic tryptophan. It has sufficient molecule and reaction counts, clear hub molecules, multiple convergent branches, and good hydrothermal-to-surface-to-biochemical interconnection. It also correctly centers the unsolved indole bottleneck and includes the two most relevant literature-grounded concepts: exogenous/interstellar indole and the Ménez Fe-rich saponite hydrothermal hypothesis. However, several critical reactions are too speculative to support approval. The largest issues are rxn_011, which conflates exogenous indole availability with an unresolved terrestrial pyrrole-plus-benzene annulation, rxn_012, which assigns a prebiotic source to indole-3-acetaldehyde despite the literature stating none is known, and rxn_016, which proposes a direct indole-serine condensation despite the literature emphasizing why even biology requires sophisticated enzyme-mediated channeling for this chemistry. There is also some environmental mismatch in treating exogenous delivery as a conventional surface reaction node and in using generic hydrothermal pyruvate/formaldehyde chemistry without detailed literature-linked constraints. Overall, the graph is a high-quality hypothesis network but not a high-confidence viable synthesis network for tryptophan. It should not be approved under the stated threshold.
